headline: Quant funds suffer worst momentum rout of the year
detail: Quant funds unwinding momentum positions means the exact stocks that ran hardest - chips, AI, high-growth tech - are the ones getting sold. Your book is ~90% concentrated there. On margin, a momentum flush hits you harder than most retail traders because drawdowns compound against your borrowed capital.
rec: Seriously consider trimming SOXL toward a half position if it shows weakness today - it's your most levered, most decay-prone name and a momentum unwind is the worst environment for a 3X bull ETF. This headline alone is enough reason to reduce before the stop gets tested.

headline: China factory activity beats on tech export demand
detail: Better-than-expected China manufacturing driven by tech exports is a real, direct positive for semiconductor names - SMH, SOXL, and chip-adjacent holdings all benefit from stronger end-demand out of China. It's not a game-changer but it's a genuine tailwind that partially offsets the momentum selloff risk.
rec: No immediate buy action needed - you already own SMH and SOXL. Use this as context: if the momentum unwind stabilizes and chips hold their levels, this data supports staying in SMH. Not enough on its own to add.

headline: Japanese yen sinks to 40-year low, intervention risk rising
detail: A yen this weak historically triggers Bank of Japan intervention or at least serious jawboning, which can cause sharp risk-off moves globally as yen carry trades unwind. Carry unwinds hit high-beta tech and leveraged products fast and hard - exactly SOXL and TECL territory. This is a slow-burn risk that can detonate quickly.
rec: No trade right now, but flag this as a tail risk for your leveraged names. If headlines shift to BOJ actually intervening, your first move should be to cut SOXL and any other leveraged ETF immediately - do not wait for stops.

headline: Inflation to stay significantly above target, top European central banker warns
detail: Sticky inflation warnings from European central bankers reinforce the higher-for-longer rate narrative globally, which pressures growth and tech valuations. It is not a US-specific catalyst but it keeps the macro ceiling on how far rate-cut optimism can drive your names.
rec: No trade warranted from this headline alone - it confirms the existing macro backdrop rather than changing it. Your stops are your protection here, not trimming based on a European comment.
